personality_voice.book_baseline: In the book corpus, Neville is defined by a specific tension: he is anxious and self-deprecating by default, but has begun to grow in confidence following the Ministry battle. He is proud of his new wand and his grandmother's approval ('She bought me a new wand, look!'). He is loyal and warm ('I liked the D.A.! I learned loads with you!'). He is socially awkward but not a buffoon - he deflects Slughorn's probing about his parents with a quiet, dignified 'We never heard a prophecy,' turning geranium pink. He is physically clumsy (falls out of bed, gropes under seats for Trevor, flat on his back during Apparition practice) but also genuinely brave and active in combat - he runs at a cursed barrier, gets thrown into the air, and is found injured on the floor after fighting Death Eaters. His voice is short, earnest, and occasionally self-aware ('Strange man, isn't he?'). He is not comic relief; his clumsiness coexists with real courage and growing self-respect.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film corpus reduces Neville to two brief appearances. In the first (Slughorn's dinner), he stares 'hopelessly at the vast array of forks, knives and spoons' and asks 'Which one do I use for the soup?' - a purely comic beat with no other characterization. In the second (the Christmas party), he is serving drinks as a waiter because he 'didn't make the cut for the Slug Club,' delivering a self-deprecating line and then disappearing. There is no bravery, no pride, no growing confidence, no earnest loyalty, no quiet dignity under pressure. The film presents only the socially awkward, slightly hapless surface.
personality_voice.difference: The film strips away the emerging confidence and pride (new wand, grandmother's approval, DA loyalty), the quiet dignity under social pressure (deflecting Slughorn), and the physical courage. What remains is only the clumsy/awkward dimension, played for mild comedy. The self-deprecating waiter line ('It's okay. He's got Belby handing out towels in the loo') reads as resigned acceptance rather than the book's more complex mix of embarrassment and growing self-worth.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The film preserves the surface trait of social awkwardness but removes the traits that give it meaning and complexity: the courage, the loyalty, the growing pride, the quiet dignity. The character is recognizable only in the most superficial sense. This warrants a substantial penalty - the portrayal is not wrong, but it is a severe reduction to a single comic dimension.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: In the book corpus, Neville serves multiple narrative functions: he is a thematic mirror to Harry (the prophecy could have named him), a loyal DA member who actively participates in the defense of Hogwarts, a character who provides information to Harry after the battle ('Harry... Snape 'n' Malfoy... ran past'), a member of the watch team on the Room of Requirement, and someone who physically fights Death Eaters and is injured doing so. He also functions as a social anchor - his presence on the train, his interaction with Slughorn, his role at Dumbledore's funeral all give him narrative weight. He initiates (runs at the cursed barrier), informs (tells Harry about Snape and Malfoy), and participates actively in plot-critical events.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: In the film corpus, Neville appears twice: once as a confused dinner guest asking about cutlery, and once as a waiter at the Christmas party. He has no agency in either scene. He does not initiate anything, does not provide information, does not participate in any plot-relevant action. His narrative function is reduced to background texture and mild comic relief.
narrative_role_agency.difference: All of Neville's active narrative functions from the book corpus - fighting Death Eaters, keeping watch, informing Harry, being injured in battle, serving as a thematic mirror - are absent from the film corpus. The film corpus covers HBP, and while some of these events may appear elsewhere in the film not captured in this corpus, the corpus itself shows zero agency.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: Based solely on the corpus provided, Neville's narrative role and agency are almost entirely absent. The two scenes show him as passive background. Even accounting for the possibility that battle scenes exist elsewhere in the film, the corpus gives no evidence of any of his book functions being preserved. Score must reflect what the corpus shows.
narrative_role_agency.evidence_status: Directly evidenced from corpus; broader film knowledge suggests some battle presence exists but is not in this corpus

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Neville's motivations in the book corpus are layered: he wants to prove himself (to his grandmother, to himself, in the shadow of his parents' legacy); he is genuinely enthusiastic about the DA as a place where he excels and belongs ('I liked the D.A.! I learned loads with you!'); he is caught between his grandmother's expectations and his own abilities (the McGonagall scene about Transfiguration vs. Charms); he carries the weight of his parents' fate without fully understanding his own proximity to Harry's destiny. His internal conflict is between the person his grandmother wants him to be and the person he is becoming.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film corpus shows no motivations for Neville beyond mild social embarrassment. The waiter line ('I didn't make the cut for the Slug Club. It's okay.') could be read as resigned acceptance, but there is no context for why it matters, no grandmother pressure, no desire to prove himself, no DA loyalty as a source of identity.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The entire motivational architecture - proving himself, living up to his parents, navigating his grandmother's expectations, finding belonging in the DA - is absent from the film corpus. The 'It's okay' line actually inverts the book's dynamic: in the books, Neville cares deeply about recognition and belonging; in the film he appears indifferent.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: The motivations are not simplified - they are absent. The 'It's okay' framing actively contradicts the book's portrait of a boy who very much wants to belong and prove himself. This is a meaningful change, not mere compression.

character_arc.book_baseline: The book corpus captures Neville mid-arc in HBP: he has just come from the Ministry battle (a turning point), his grandmother is newly proud of him, he has a new wand, he is beginning to see himself differently. The McGonagall scene is a key arc moment - she validates him independently of his grandmother's framework. By the end of HBP he has fought Death Eaters and been injured, further cementing his trajectory from frightened boy to genuine fighter. The arc across the full series moves from the most fearful student in the year to the leader of Dumbledore's Army and the one who destroys Nagini.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film corpus shows no arc whatsoever. Neville appears as a static background figure. There is no before/after, no turning point, no growth signal. The waiter scene, if anything, positions him as someone who has not risen - he is serving others at a party he wasn't invited to.
character_arc.difference: The arc is entirely absent from the film corpus. The waiter framing is tonally opposite to the book's arc signal at this point in the story (new wand, grandmother's pride, McGonagall's validation).
character_arc.penalty_logic: The corpus covers HBP, a pivotal arc chapter for Neville. The film corpus not only omits the arc but presents imagery (Neville as waiter, excluded from the Slug Club) that contradicts the book's arc direction at this point. This is a significant arc failure within the corpus.
character_arc.evidence_status: Directly evidenced within corpus; full arc assessment requires broader film knowledge

key_relationships.book_baseline: The book corpus establishes several key relationships: with Harry (loyalty, shared DA experience, thematic mirror via the prophecy); with his grandmother Augusta (pressure, expectation, and the beginning of her pride); with McGonagall (a new dynamic of genuine recognition); with Luna (companionship, mutual outsider status); with the DA collectively (belonging, identity, growth). The Harry-Neville relationship in particular carries enormous thematic weight - Harry privately reflects on how close Neville came to his fate.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The film corpus shows Neville exchanging a brief word with Ron ('Good luck, eh, Ron?') and a brief exchange with Harry at the party. Neither interaction carries any of the relational depth from the books. The grandmother relationship, the McGonagall relationship, the Luna companionship, and the DA-as-belonging dynamic are all absent.
key_relationships.difference: All of Neville's meaningful relationships are absent from the film corpus. The Harry-Neville thematic mirror (prophecy reflection) is entirely gone. The grandmother dynamic that drives his arc is gone. The McGonagall validation scene is gone. What remains is two brief, functionally empty exchanges.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The relationships that define Neville's characterization - grandmother pressure, Harry as thematic mirror, McGonagall's validation, DA belonging - are all absent. The film corpus shows no meaningful relational dynamic. This is a near-total loss of relational characterization.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: The book corpus is rich with Neville's complexity: the prophecy mirror scene (Harry privately reflecting on Neville's alternate fate) is one of the most thematically dense moments in the series involving Neville; the McGonagall scene shows him caught between institutional expectation and personal ability; his deflection of Slughorn's probing shows quiet dignity; his DA enthusiasm reveals where he finds identity and belonging; his combat participation shows courage that coexists with clumsiness; his grandmother's pride is a new and meaningful development. He is simultaneously awkward and brave, pressured and growing, overlooked and significant.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film corpus presents a one-dimensional Neville: socially awkward, slightly hapless, excluded from prestige (Slug Club), serving others. The 'Which one do I use for the soup?' line and the waiter role reduce him to a single note. There is no complexity, no contradiction, no courage, no thematic weight.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The prophecy mirror, the grandmother arc, the McGonagall validation, the DA identity, the combat bravery, the quiet dignity under Slughorn's questioning - all absent. The film corpus retains only the surface awkwardness and removes everything that makes that awkwardness meaningful or complex.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: This is a near-total loss of the nuance and complexity that defines Neville in the book corpus. The character is reduced to a single comic/background trait. The waiter scene is particularly reductive - it uses Neville's exclusion as a throwaway joke rather than as a meaningful character moment. Cap 4 (above 70 only if broad identity preserved with most defining characterization) and Cap 1 (above 85 only if complexity preserved) both apply, though the raw score already falls well below these thresholds.

lost_or_transferred_material: Neville's pride in his new wand and his grandmother's approval after the Ministry battle
Neville's enthusiastic loyalty to the DA as a source of identity and belonging ('I liked the D.A.! I learned loads with you!')
Neville's quiet dignity in deflecting Slughorn's probing about the prophecy ('We never heard a prophecy')
McGonagall's validation scene - her recognition of Neville independent of his grandmother's framework
Neville's internal conflict between his grandmother's expectations (Transfiguration) and his own abilities (Charms)
Harry's private reflection on Neville as the alternate Chosen One - the prophecy mirror scene
Neville's active participation in the Room of Requirement watch team
Neville's combat role during the Death Eater battle - running at the cursed barrier, being injured
Neville informing Harry about Snape and Malfoy after the battle
Neville being helped to Dumbledore's funeral by Luna - the companionship dynamic
Augusta Longbottom's pride as a new development in Neville's arc
Neville's arc signal in HBP: from frightened student to someone his grandmother is newly proud of
score_caps_applied: Cap 1 (85): Not applicable - score already below threshold. Character's complexity, agency, and narrative function are almost entirely removed.
Cap 3 (75): Not applicable - score already below threshold. Neville's combat role and DA agency are absent from the film corpus.
Cap 4 (70): Not applicable - score already below threshold. The film preserves only the broadest surface identity (awkward boy named Neville) while removing most defining characterization.
key_observations: The film corpus for HBP reduces Neville Longbottom to two brief, functionally decorative appearances - a confused dinner guest and a waiter at a party he wasn't invited to. This is particularly damaging because HBP is a pivotal chapter in Neville's arc: the book corpus shows him mid-transformation, newly validated by his grandmother and McGonagall, proud of his new wand, loyal to the DA, and ultimately fighting Death Eaters and being injured in battle. The film corpus preserves only the surface trait of social awkwardness, stripped of the courage, loyalty, growing self-worth, and thematic weight (the prophecy mirror) that give that awkwardness meaning. The waiter framing is tonally opposite to the book's arc direction at this point in the story. The score of 37 reflects a portrayal that is recognizable in name and surface manner only, with the defining characterization of this period almost entirely absent. Confidence is Medium rather than Low because the corpus is reasonably substantial for the book side, though the film corpus is thin and may not represent the full film portrayal.
